verse_specific_anchors: the ocean metaphor (āpūryamāṇam acala-pratiṣṭham samudram āpaḥ praviśanti) is verbatim 2.70's contribution; no other Gita verse uses this exact rivers-into-ocean image with the full-and-unmoving qualifier
the kāma-kāmī reduplicative compound is unique to 2.70; named explicitly in the engineering layer with its reduplicative force preserved

the receiving-without-overflow distinction (vs impassivity / refusing input) is licensed by the verse's specific structural claim about the ocean's nature; this distinction is what makes the verse not a blanket call to detachment
objection: The analog could not equally apply to 2.55 (Arjuna's question), 2.56 (the sage unmoved by sukha-duḥkha), 2.57 (the sage neither attached nor averse), 2.58 (the tortoise verse), or 2.71 (the four-fold disposition leading to peace). 2.56 is closest in spirit (the sage unmoved) but lacks the receiving-without-refusing structural force; 2.71 is adjacent but uses the four-fold negation structure rather than the ocean metaphor. 2.70's five-river morning analog with explicit input-receiving is anchored in the ocean's structural property, not in general 'be calm' advice. Pass.
